title: Lesson 270
transcript: Within risk analysis, we've talked about how there's two major methods that we can use to rank the risks, qualitative and quantitative. And for quantitative analysis, monetary analysis, there's one formula that you need to know about. It's known as the ALE calculation, annualized loss expectancy. Let's go through that calculation. And before we go into it, the whole point of the annualized loss expectancy calculation is to determine exactly how much would a risk cost us annually per year in some sort of monetary values, Canadian pesos. Now, in order to calculate the ALE, the annualized loss expectancy, we're actually gonna start with the SLE, single loss expectancy. The SLE is how much we're going to lose if the risk occurs once. That's our SLE. Now, in order to calculate the SLE, there's actually two numbers that we have to look at. The first of which is what's known as the asset value. And the asset value is simply how much is the asset worth? So let's use an example here. Let's talk about, say, my laptop. And we'll say that the hardware of my laptop, that's $1,000. And the data that I have on my laptop is another $1,000. So what is my asset value? $1,000 plus $1,000, $2,000. And that highlights something important about questions you'll see on the ALE calculation on the exam. The math is not difficult. You're not gonna need your TI-83 graphing calculator for any of these calculations. What is going to be difficult is picking out the relevant numbers. It's not uncommon that you'll see these as part of a scenario based question. They'll give you a whole bunch of information and it's your job to pick out what exactly is the asset value? What's the exposure factor, the ARO? it's difficult to pick out exactly what they're asking you about. What are the key numbers that you need to use? So make sure you really understand the definitions of what these numbers are, because that's truly what you need to know here. Don't worry about having to do any difficult math, okay? So our asset value is simply how much is our asset worth. And this is where we actually encounter the first problem of why it's so difficult to do the ALE calculation. There's a lot of assets where it's very difficult to calculate exactly how much they're worth. Like my laptop. I said the hardware was worth $1,000. That's very easy. You can easily look up what's the cost to buy a new laptop. But I said my data was worth $1,000. That's a lot harder to calculate. How much exactly is my data worth? I'm giving you an IC's number, $1,000. You know, all these slides on here, all this work that I've put into creating all these presentations, that's probably at least five bucks, right? I don't know. Very difficult to calculate. So our asset value, how much is our asset worth? The next number you need to know is what's known as the exposure factor, and it's a percentage between 0 and 100%. An exposure factor is a measure of what percentage of our asset are we going to lose if the risk occurs. So if our exposure factor is 0%, we don't lose any of the asset. And if it's 100%, then we lose all of our asset. So what if I told you that I backed up all the data with my laptop and then my laptop was lost? What would be my exposure factor? Well, I obviously would have lost the hardware and the data that was on that laptop, but my data was backed up. So really, how much of the value of my laptop did I lose? Only 50%. So this is kind of what you're looking for with exposure factor. You're looking for the percentage of something that is lost if the risk occurs. And this is again often very difficult to calculate. Like let's say that your risk that you're concerned about is a fire. What percentage of the burning of the building would burn down if there was a fire? Was it 12% or 37%? It can often be very difficult to come up with your exposure factor. But let's say in this example I'm giving you, our exposure factor is 50%. So our asset value was $2,000. We multiply that by our exposure factor, 50%, and that gives us our single loss expectancy. How much are we going to lose if my laptop is lost once? So $2,000 times 50% is... $1,000. Our SLE is $1,000. Now, in order to figure out the ALE, there's one more number we have to look at, and that is what's known as the ARO, the annualized rate of occurrence, or in other words, how many times do we expect this risk to occur annually per year? So, if you expect the risk to occur once every year, then the AIRO is going to be 1. If we expect the risk to occur twice a year, then the AIRO is going to be 2. Okay, for the math geniuses out there, if the risk is going to occur every two years, what's a ARO? 0.5. So again, math, not complicated. But again, coming up with the ARO can be often very difficult. How often do your employees lose their laptops? What's the chance of an earthquake? What's the chance of a plane landing on your building? It can often be very difficult to come up with the ARO. Where can you get this data from? Well, if you've got historical records, often insurance companies keep actuarial tables. Those are some places you could try and find this data, but often difficult to find. Now, from my own historical experience, I lose my laptop on average about twice a year. So let's say the ARO is 2. Okay, so asset value, $2,000. Exposure factor, 50%. Means our SLE is $1,000 times 2. Means that our annualized loss expectancy is... two thousand dollars so there you go that's the a le calculation very simple math sometimes difficult to figure out what the numbers are and one other really important thing to watch out for on the exam they sometimes use different terminology for these different things not to confuse you but because in the industry there isn't general consensus on exactly what to call all of these things. So the exam will use different terminology that different frameworks out there use. So for example, the ALE might be referred to as, what would your potential loss be? And your SLE might be the impact of an event, because SLE is a single event. And your arrow, for instance, might be frequency of occurrence. So watch out for that similar type of wording that means the same sort of thing.
